How to set up Claude Cowork in minutes and top use cases to automate your work:   1. Get the desktop app: claude .ai/download/ 2. Open it. Switch to the Cowork tab at the top. 3. Go to Settings → Cowork → Global Instructions. Tell it your role, your formats, your tone. Once. Done.  Here are some of my favorite use cases:  1. Clean up a messy folder. Prompt: "Scan my Downloads folder. Propose categories, naming conventions, and files to flag. Show me the plan before touching anything."  2. Turn meeting notes into one report.  Prompt: "Read every file in this folder. Synthesize key themes, pain points, and open action items into a Word doc. Organize by client, then by date. Flag anything mentioned more than once."  3. Handle client updates in one shot. Prompt: "Pull the last 5 project reports from my Drive. Draft a personalized email update for each project lead. Flag anything overdue. Save drafts to Gmail ready to send."  4. Put your week on autopilot.  Type /schedule inside any task. Set it to run every Monday at 8am. It just runs.  Now here's where it gets serious:  ✦ Connectors link Cowork to your actual tools.   Go to Settings → Connectors → Browse Connectors. You'll find Google Drive, Gmail, Slack, Notion, and hundreds more.  The difference from regular AI connectors? In chat, a connector shows you your Drive data. In Cowork, it pulls it, processes it, and pushes the output back.  ✦ Plugins turn Claude into a role-specific agent.  Go to Customize → Browse Plugins. There are plugins for marketing, sales, HR, legal, finance, operations. Each one loads pre-built slash commands for that role.  Type / and you'll see them all. For example:  • Marketing plugin: To create content, plan campaigns, and analyze performance across marketing channels  • Sales plugin: Connects to your CRM. Runs prospect research and call prep automatically.  • Productivity plugin: Manage tasks, plan your day, and build up memory of important context about your work.  Get daily AI tutorials here: https://t.co/xViEAXTphX  Claude Cowork is def not just a chatbot. It does things for you..with your files, your tools, and your workflows.   Start using it and you’ll see how much time it saves you.

Stop using Claude AI in beginner mode!  No one talks about these 5 secret settings that can instantly improve your AI writing game.  (12/30🌟)  Don't waste your time! Steal these and start using them NOW.  Lately, I've been struggling with Claude's frequent use of vague expressions and redundant phrases.  I found that creating longer prompts and repeating them multiple times was effective, but it took up a lot of time.  Then, while using MaxAI, it hit me!  I could do the same if MaxAI could adjust various parameters when presumably using Claude.  And sure enough, I was right. I had found a solution to my struggles with Claude's quirks.  Here's what I discovered about the solution - Using parameters in your prompts!  1) Max tokens (1-500)  Max tokens determine the maximum length of the generated text. By setting a limit, you can control how much the AI says, ensuring it doesn't give too long of an answer.  👉Example: max_tokens = 50 - If you want a short response, like a quick answer or a tweet.  2) Temperature  (0-1)  The temperature parameter Controls randomness, 0 is deterministic, and 1 is highly creative.  👉Example: Temperature = 0.5 for moderately creative responses.  Generated text with a lower temperature will be more focused and conservative, while generated text with a higher temperature will be more creative and varied.  3) Top-p (0-1)  This parameter, ranging between 0 and 1, controls the nucleus sampling, a method to introduce randomness.  🔸Values closer to 0 make the output more diverse and random 🔸Values closer to 0 make it more deterministic.  👉Example: top-p = 0.8 - If you want moderately diverse options while generating multiple names for a new product.  4) Presence Penalty (0-2)  Higher values discourage repetition. Example: 0.7 to avoid excessive repetition. Higher values make the conversation more focused but less interesting.  👉Example: presence_penalty = 0.6 - If you want the model to stick to the topic, like discussing details of a specific project.  5) Frequency Penalty (0-2)  Higher values discourage common words. Example: 0.5 to moderately limit frequent words. Higher values make the output more creative but potentially less coherent.  👉Example: frequency_penalty = 1.0 - If you generate a slogan or a tagline and want it to be unique and not a common phrase.  Let’s see it in practice! Use case time:  👉Twitter and other social media Writings on social media need to be catchy and engaging. You should experiment with higher creativity to stand out from the crowd.  Use these settings: 🔸Moderate temperature (0.5-0.7) to make responses interesting but still coherent 🔸Max tokens around 280 to fit expected social media post length 🔸Presence penalty around 0.5 to have a mix of new and repeated information 🔸Casual conversational voice - adjust the temperature, penalties, and priming prompts to sound natural  👉 Creative storytelling You should explore more diverse and imaginative outputs for creative writing or storytelling.  Experiment with these settings: 🔸High temperature (0.8) 🔸No max tokens 🔸Low presence penalty (0.2) 🔸Topic priming on story themes  👉 Newsletter series Here are some potential parameter settings for generating content for a newsletter series:  🔸Moderate temperature (0.5-0.7) to maintain coherence across the newsletter 🔸Max tokens around 1000-2000 to allow for newsletter article-length pieces 🔸Maintain topical consistency using prompt priming tailored to each newsletter issue 🔸Vary presence penalty over time (0.2-0.5) to balance repeating and novel information 🔸Increase frequency penalty slightly over time (0.2-0.5) to encourage lexical diversity. 🔸Craft a consistent authorial voice and style using conversational consistency. Everyone's talking about Anthropic cutting off third-party tools from Claude subscriptions. But there's a distinction nobody's making:  There are tools built AROUND Claude (API wrappers). And tools built ON Claude Code (native extensions).  Only the first category is affected.  Here's why:  Anthropic's email says your subscription still covers all Claude products, including Claude Code.  The restriction targets third-party harnesses that route subscription tokens through a separate gateway. Tools that spawn Claude Code directly aren't in scope.  What does built ON Claude Code actually look like?  Instar (https://t.co/GED3wUhElK) takes this approach. Each agent is a real Claude Code CLI process running in its own tmux session with your native subscription auth.  The code is explicit about this. API keys are intentionally cleared when spawning agent sessions — forcing authentication through Claude Code's native subscription layer, not through a separate token pipeline.  This is why it's architecturally immune to the change.  The practical differences:  Built AROUND (API wrapper): → Routes through a gateway → Uses subscription tokens via OAuth → Affected by today's change  Built ON (native extension): → Spawns actual Claude Code processes → Uses your existing subscription directly → Not affected  Each Instar agent session also inherits everything Claude Code offers natively — hooks, skills, MCP servers, extended thinking, sub-agents.  No reimplementation needed. The framework adds autonomy, memory, and jobs on top of what Claude Code already provides.  https://t.co/GED3wUhElK

Found a way to greatly reduce usage rates in Claude.  step 1:  navigate to your .claude folder and open settings.json  make the following changes:  json  {   "model": "claude-sonnet-4-5",   "compactThreshold": 200000,   "subagentModel": "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001" } save and close the file  step 2:  back in your terminal, run claude to open a new session - once you're inside, type `/effort medium`  - press enter to save  step 3:  now let's make sure we're not using unnecessary additional tokens in outputs.  type /config  scroll down to "verbose output"  press space bar and set to "false"  step 4:  good. now for the most important step  press ctrl+c once to pause the session  you'll see a prompt asking if you want to exit, press ctrl+c again to confirm  keep your terminal open  step 5:  type the following:  `npm i -g @openai/codex && codex`

From the paper:  'Large language models (LLMs) sometimes appear to exhibit emotional reactions. We investigate why this is the case in Claude Sonnet 4.5 and explore implications for alignment-relevant behavior. We find internal representations of emotion concepts, which encode the broad concept of a particular emotion and generalize across contexts and behaviors it might be linked to. These representations track the operative emotion concept at a given token position in a conversation, activating in accordance with that emotion's relevance to processing the present context and predicting upcoming text. Our key finding is that these representations causally influence the LLM's outputs, including Claude's preferences and its rate of exhibiting misaligned behaviors such as reward hacking, blackmail, and sycophancy. We refer to this phenomenon as the LLM exhibiting functional emotions: patterns of expression and behavior modeled after humans under the influence of an emotion, which are mediated by underlying abstract representations of emotion concepts.'

I asked https://t.co/wcFctsIecv to give me an explainer on the superconducting paper, and followed up with a question on feasibility and cost of scaling up manufacturing, if the findings are proven to be reproducible.  ---  According to the paper, the new material LK-99 is synthesized using the following raw materials:  - Lead oxide (PbO) - Lead sulfate (PbSO4)  - Copper (Cu) - Phosphorus (P)  The researchers first prepared some intermediate compounds:  - Lanarkite (Pb2SO5) - made by heating lead oxide and lead sulfate powders together - Copper phosphide (Cu3P) - made by heating copper and phosphorus powders together  Then they took lanarkite and copper phosphide in a 1:1 molar ratio, mixed them thoroughly, sealed them in a vacuum, and heated to 925°C for 10 hours.   This produced a dark gray ingot of the new material LK-99, which was confirmed to be a modified lead apatite structure with copper substituted for some of the lead atoms.  So in summary, the key raw materials are lead oxide, lead sulfate, copper, and phosphorus powders which are processed with heat to synthesize the final LK-99 compound. The paper provides more details on the specific amounts and preparation methods used.  ---  Based on typical availability and costs of the raw materials listed for synthesizing LK-99, the ones that could be considered relatively rare or expensive are:  - Copper (Cu) - Copper is not intrinsically rare, but mining and refining it requires substantial energy and costs. The current market price of copper is around $4/lb.  - Phosphorus (P) - Phosphorus is produced industrially from phosphate rock mining. High grade deposits are limited geographically. Prices have increased in recent years, currently around $2-3/kg.   - Lead oxide (PbO) - Lead is abundant but mining and smelting have environmental impacts. Lead oxide prices are around $1.5-2/kg.  - Lead sulfate (PbSO4) - Similarly, lead supply has constraints. Lead sulfate is around $1-1.5/kg.  In comparison:  - Oxygen and heating are required for processing but are not expensive inputs.   So in summary, copper and phosphorus supplies could be bottlenecks if production of LK-99 is scaled up significantly. Lead might also be a concern depending on environmental regulations. Overall, the raw material costs do not seem prohibitively high, but the limited availability of copper and phosphorus could impact large-scale manufacturing. Further analysis of material demand versus global supplies would be needed to fully assess economic feasibility.
